content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFO RNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL P RACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Wayne Craig 
506 S Broadway #A 
Redondo Beach, CA 902 77 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-181 
Dear Mr. Craig: 
October 6, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice as treasurer of Rescue our Waterfront PAC 
("ROW PAC") regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the provisions of the Act and not under 
other general laws outside the Act. Additionally, the Commission does not provide advice relating 
to past conduct, or actions currently the subject of a complaint that is under investigation by the Fair 
Political Practices Commission's Enforcement Division. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A) and (F).) 
Therefore, any conclusions contained herein apply only to prospective actions and do not apply to 
issues raised in the Enforcement complaint against ROW PAC noted below. 
QUESTIONS 
1. May ROW PAC receive contributions and make expenditures for its legal defense in a civil 
lawsuit that relates to its status as a general purpose committee?2 
2. Must funds raised by ROW PAC be used only for the committee and its individual officers' 
legal expenses? 
3. May ROW PAC donate or make a gift of campaign funds towards the legal expenses of 
other individuals named in the lawsuit? 
4. May ROW PAC coordinate fundraising activities with other political action committees for 
legal expenses? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
? A general purpose committee is a committee fonned primarily to support or oppose more than one candidate 
or ballot measure. (Section 82027.S) 


CONCLUSIONS 
FileNo. A-17-181 
Page No. 2 
1. Yes. ROW PAC may receive contributions for its legal defense, subject to the Act and local 
contribution laws. Campaign fund expenditures may be made for these legal expenses which 
arise directly out of ROW PAC's activities and involve the defense of an action brought for 
a violation of campaign and disclosure laws. 
2. Yes. The Act's definition of attorney's fees and costs pertaining to the use of campaign 
funds limits these expenditures to costs related to the defense of the committee officer(s) 
and for administrative costs directly related to compliance with the requirements of the Act. 
3. No. The Act does not permit the use of campaign funds for donations or loans to recipients 
that are not bona fide charitable, tax-exempt, nonprofit organizations. The Act also does not 
permit the payment of legal services for third parties under its definition of permissible 
legal expenses that may be paid from campaign funds. 
4. Yes, with the appropriate disclosure of each contribution's allocation and reporting of 
contributions pursuant to the Act. 
FACTS 
You are the treasurer of Rescue our Waterfront PAC ("Committee" or "ROW PAC"), a 
local recipient committee. You have provided the information contained in this statement of facts. 
ROW PAC filed with the City of Redondo Beach as a ••general purpose" committee in its 
Statement of Organization Form 410 in July 2016, and in its subsequent Recipient Committee 
Campaign Statements, Form 460. ROW PAC endorsed multiple candidates as well as local ballot 
Measure C in the recent March 2017 election, pursuant to the committee's mission statement. You 
state that ROW PAC's largest expenditures were towards Measure C, but did not exceed 43% of its 
expenditures in connection with the relevant election. 
ROW PAC is currently named in a civil complaint filed by two individuals, Chris Voisey 
and Arnette Travis, alleging the that ROW PAC violated campaign disclosure requirements under 
the Act, should be classified as a primarily formed ballot measure committee3 related to local 
Ballot Measure C and that the committee was controlled by candidates Bill Brand, a candidate for 
mayor, and Nils Nehrenheim, a candidate for city council. The lawsuit named ROW PAC's 
individual officers, yourself and Martin Holmes; Bill Brand, mayor of Redondo Beach; his 
campaign treasurer, Linda Moffat; and council member Nils Nehrenheim as defendants. 
3 A primarily fonned committee is a committee fonned or existing primarily to support or oppose any of the 
following: (a) a single candidale; (b) a single measure; (c) a group of specific candidates in lhe same city, county, or 
multicounty election; (d) two or more measures being voted on in the same city, county or multicounty election. 
(Section 82047.5.) 


ANALYSIS 
Use of Campaign Funds under the Act 
FileNo. A-17-181 
Page No. 3 
To prevent candidates, elected officials, and others who control the expenditure of campaign 
funds from benefiting privately from their campaign activities, the Act regulates how campaign 
funds may be spent. The general rule is that an expenditure of campaign funds must be reasonably 
related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section 89511.) 
Where an expenditure of campaign funds confers a "substantial personal benefit"4 on any 
individual or individuals with authority to approve the expenditure of campaign funds held by the 
committee, the expenditure is held to a higher standard and must be directly related to a political, 
legislative, or governmental purpose. (Sections 89511 and 89512.5.) This higher standard of a 
"direct relationship" applies if the expenditure is for specific activities such as: travel, professional 
services, specialty clothing, fines, tickets for sporting or entertainment events, or personal gifts. 
(Section 89513.) 
Professional Services: Attorney Fees and Costs 
Section 89513(b)(l )  states that campaign funds may not be used to reimburse the cost of 
professional services unless the services are directly related to a political, legislative, or 
governmental purpose. Section 89514 delineates that attorney fees and costs are "directly related" 
in those instances where the litigation is directly related to the activities of a committee that are 
consistent with its primary objectives; or arises directly out of a committee's activities. This section 
lists the "defense of an action brought for a violation of state or local campaign or disclosure laws" 
as an example of a "directly related" action. Section 89514 states: 
Expenditures of campaign funds for attorney's fees and other costs in connection 
with administrative, civil, or criminal litigation are not directly related to a 
political, legislative, or governmental purpose except where the litigation is 
directly related to activities of a committee that are consistent with its primary 
objectives or arises directly out of a committee's activities or out of a candidate's 
or elected officer's activities, duties, or status as a candidate or elected officer, 
including, but not limited to, an action to enjoin defamation, defense of an action 
to enjoin defamation, defense of an action brought for a violation of state or local 
campaign, disclosure, or election laws, and an action arising from an election 
contest or recount. 
The Commission has addressed when this standard is met in several advice letters (See e.g., 
Lanning Advice Letter, No. A-92-05, defense of a conflict-of-interest charge; Richter Advice 
Letter, No. 1-93-355, legal advice prior to commencement of an action; Bagatelos Advice Letter, 
No. A-94-091, defense of a complaint lodged with the Federal Elections Commission; Dale Advice 
Letter No. A-15-201, the defense of a Section 1090 civil action.) The Commission also advised that 
4 Section 8951 l(b)(3): "substantial personal benefit" means an expenditure of campaign funds which results in 
a direct personal benefit with a value of more than two hundred dollars ($200) to a candidate, elected officer, or any 
individual or individuals with authority to approve the expenditure of campaign funds held by a committee. 


FileNo. A-17-181 
Page No. 4 
the standard was not met where the action related to employee benefits and not the employee's 
status as an elected officer. (Breit/elder Advice Letter, No. A-95-058, accusation of falsifying a city 
government health insurance document.) 
Further, the Act limits the definition of .. attorney fees and costs" that may be expended. 
Section 8951 l(b)(S)(A) defines "attorney's fees and other costs" for purposes of the use of 
campaign funds to include only the following: 
(i) Attorney's fees and other legal costs related to the defense of the 
candidate or officer. 
(ii) Administrative costs directly related to compliance with the 
requirements of this title. 
Please note that Section 895 l l(b)(5)(8) specifically excludes from this definition 
expenses for fundraising: 
"Attorney's fees and other costs" does not include expenses for 
fundraising, media or political consulting fees, mass mailing or other advertising, 
or, except as expressly authorized by subdivision (c) of Section 89513, a payment 
or reimbursement for a fine, penalty, judgment or settlement, or a payment to 
return or disgorge contributions made to any other committee controlled by the 
candidate or officer. 
In this matter, the civil lawsuit relates to recent campaign statements filed by ROW PAC, its 
designation on those forms, and its expenditures. This litigation arises directly out of the 
committee's activities and in defense of an action brought for a violation of campaign and 
disclosure laws. Therefore, ROW PAC and its officers' attorney fees and costs for this action are 
directly related to a political and governmental purpose. The Act does not prohibit you from using 
campaign funds to pay for the legal services applicable to the ROW PAC and its officers, within the 
definition stated in Section 89511 (b )(S)(A) and (8). The defense of others is not included in the 
definition of attorney's fees and costs. 
Gift, Loan or Donation to Third Party Individuals for Legal Expenses 
You also inquire as to whether ROW PAC may donate or contribute (make a gift of) 
campaign funds for a third-party individual's (Mr. Nehrenheim, Mr. Brand, and Ms. Moffat, Mr. 
Brand's campaign treasurer) legal expenses. A gift is defined as any payment that confers a 
personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not 
received. (Section 82028.) Section 89513(t)(l) permits the use of campaign funds for gifts under 
the higher standard; i.e., where there is a direct relationship between the expenditure for the gifts 
and a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. 
As stated above, ROW PAC may only expend campaign funds for the attorney's fees and 
other costs related to the defense of its officers or controlling candidates. Presuming that ROW 


File No. A-17-181 
Page No. 5 
PAC takes the position that it was not a controlled committee,5 the payment by ROW PAC would 
not be considered legitimate attorney fees and costs related to the committee, and the payments 
would be an impermissible gift of funds. 
Nor does the Act permit ROW PAC to make a donation or loan of campaign funds to these 
individuals. ROW PAC may make donations or loans only to organizations that meet the 
requirements stated in Sections 89513(g) or 89515. Section 89513(g) states: 
Campaign funds shall not be used to make loans other than to organizations 
pursuant to Section 89515, or, unless otherwise prohibited, to a candidate for 
elective office, political party, or committee. 
Section 89515 provides that campaign funds may be used to make donations or loans to bona fide 
charitable organizations, under the following conditions: 
Campaign funds may be used to make donations or loans to bona fide charitable, 
educational, civic, religious, or similar true-exempt, nonprofit organizations, where 
no substantial part of the proceeds will have a material financial effect on the 
candidate, elected officer, campaign treasurer, or any individual or individuals 
with authority to approve the expenditure of campaign funds held by a committee, 
or member of his or her immediate family, and where the donation or loan bears a 
reasonable relation to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. 
(Section 89515.) 
The individuals identified by ROW PAC do not meet the requirements stated in Section 
89513(g) or 89515.6 
Payment of Fines, Penalties or Judgements 
Section 89513( c )( 1) provides that campaign funds may be used to pay or reimburse fines, 
penalties, judgments, or settlements, for any action for which payment of attorney's fees from 
contributions is permitted under the Act, so long as such penalty did not relate to an expenditure of 
campaign funds that resulted in either of the following: 
(i) A personal benefit to the candidate or officer if it is determined that the 
expenditure was not reasonably related to a political, legislative, or governmental 
purpose. 
(ii) A substantial personal benefit to the candidate or officer if it is determined 
that the expenditure was not directly related to a political, legislative, or 
governmental purpose." 
5 See Section 82016, "Controlled Committee.'' 
6 We note, however, that both candidates may use funds from their own candidate election committees to pay 
for their legal defense, and ROW PAC may make contributions to those committees, under the "reasonably related" 
standard. (See Sections 89304.5 and 895 l 3(a).) However, this can raise issues of contributions by an intermediary as 
discussed further below. (See Section 84302.) 


Contributions for Legal Defense Funds 
FileNo. A-17-181 
Page No. 6 
While the Act authorizes "legal defense funds" for candidates or elected office holders, 
there is not a similar provision in the Act for other recipient committees. (See Section 85304 and 
85304.5 (local officials/candidates).) 
Joi,u Fundraiser for Legal Defense Funds 
You wish to know if ROW PAC may coordinate its legal expense fundraising with other 
political action committees. The Act does not prohibit a candidate or committee from co-hosting a 
joint fundraising event with one or more organizations. (See Goodin Advice Letter, No. A-94-290; 
Caldwell Advice Letter, No. A-A-99-252). We have advised that when a joint fundraising event is 
held, the event should be advertised under procedures which notify contributors of the specific 
amount allocated to each committee for reporting and record- keeping purposes. (See e.g., Bell 
Advice Letter No. A-11-102; and Benton Advice Letter, No. A-98-116.) Therefore, the 
advertisements for your fundraiser should make clear what percentage of the proceeds will go to 
ROW PAC and to each of the other political action committees, respectively. 
ROW PAC, and any other entity which meets the definition of a "committee" under the Act, 
is required to report the total amount of contributions received. (Sections 82013 and 84211 (a).) A 
monetary contribution is "received" on the date that the candidate or committee, or the agent of the 
candidate or committee, obtains possession or control of the check or other negotiable instrument 
by which the contribution is made. (Regulation 18421.l(c).) Therefore, when received by an agent 
of ROW PAC, the full amount of a check written for a ticket to the planned joint fundraiser would 
have to be reported as being received by ROW PAC. Any other participating political action 
committee must report its share of each ticket sold, or item donated, as a contribution. Please note 
that if ROW PAC pays more for the fundraiser than another committee it may be deemed to be 
making an in-kind contribution to the other committee; and the contribution must be reported 
accordingly. 
Additionally, the Act regulates contributions made through intermediaries: such as when a 
person makes a contribution to an organization or committee with the condition, agreement or 
understanding that the payment will be used as a contribution to another committee. (Regulation 
18432.5.) Contributions by intermediaries must be fully disclosed as made from the original 
contributor through the intermediary. Section 84302 states: 
No person shall make a contribution on behalf of another, or while acting as the 
intermediary or agent of another, without disclosing to the recipient of the 
contribution both his own full name and street address, occupation, and the name 
of his employer, if any, or his principal place of business if he is self-employed, 
and the full name and street address, occupation, and the name of employer, if 
any, or principal place of business if self-employed, of the other person. The 
recipient of the contribution shall include in his campaign statement the full name 
and street address, occupation, and the name of the employer, if any, or the 
principal place of business if self-employed, of both the intermediary and the 
contributor. 


File No. A-17-181 
Page No. 7 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
LKH:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Jack C. Woodside 
General Counsel 
Y?!{--
By: 
L. Karen Harrison 
Counsel, Legal Division

that are not bona fide charitable, tax-exempt, nonprofit organizations. The Act also does not 
permit the payment of legal services for third parties under its definition of permissible 
legal expenses that may be paid from campaign funds. 
4. Yes, with the appropriate disclosure of each contribution's allocation and reporting of 
contributions pursuant to the Act.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFO RNIA FAIR POLITICAL P RACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Wayne Craig 506 S Broadway #A Redondo Beach, CA 902 77 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-17-181 Dear Mr. Craig: October 6, 2017 This letter responds to your request for advice as treasurer of Rescue our Waterfront PAC ("ROW PAC") regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the provisions of the Act and not under other general laws outside the Act. Additionally, the Commission does not provide advice relating to past conduct, or actions currently the subject of a complaint that is under investigation by the Fair Political Practices Commission's Enforcement Division. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A) and (F).) Therefore, any conclusions contained herein apply only to prospective actions and do not apply to issues raised in the Enforcement complaint against ROW PAC noted below. QUESTIONS 1. May ROW PAC receive contributions and make expenditures for its legal defense in a civil lawsuit that relates to its status as a general purpose committee?2 2. Must funds raised by ROW PAC be used only for the committee and its individual officers' legal expenses? 3. May ROW PAC donate or make a gift of campaign funds towards the legal expenses of other individuals named in the lawsuit? 4. May ROW PAC coordinate fundraising activities with other political action committees for legal expenses? 1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. ? A general purpose committee is a committee fonned primarily to support or oppose more than one candidate or ballot measure. (Section 82027.S) CONCLUSIONS FileNo. A-17-181 Page No. 2 1. Yes. ROW PAC may receive contributions for its legal defense, subject to the Act and local contribution laws. Campaign fund expenditures may be made for these legal expenses which arise directly out of ROW PAC's activities and involve the defense of an action brought for a violation of campaign and disclosure laws. 2. Yes. The Act's definition of attorney's fees and costs pertaining to the use of campaign funds limits these expenditures to costs related to the defense of the committee officer(s) and for administrative costs directly related to compliance with the requirements of the Act. 3. No. The Act does not permit the use of campaign funds for donations or loans to recipients that are not bona fide charitable, tax-exempt, nonprofit organizations. The Act also does not permit the payment of legal services for third parties under its definition of permissible legal expenses that may be paid from campaign funds. 4. Yes, with the
